title: Chapter 30: The War Room
content: Paris opened like a wound.

Two days before Fashion Week, and the city had already rearranged itself around the coming spectacle — bollards wrapped in branded vinyl, SUVs idling at every curbside, the air thick with the particular electricity of money about to move. Jane felt it through the tinted windows of the car: the hum of a city preparing to consume itself beautifully.

The Plaza Athénée rose at the end of Avenue Montaigne like a monument to French composure, but inside, Thornfield had turned it into a hive. Jane stepped through the revolving door behind Edward and was immediately swallowed by the controlled chaos — models with clipboards, stylists arguing over steamer settings, a PR woman barking French into two phones at once. Someone pressed a champagne flute into Jane's hand. Someone else took her coat.

Edward was already ten feet ahead, shaking hands with a silver-haired man she recognized from the Financial Times. His smile was immaculate. His suit was Thornfield black, lapels sharp as paper cuts. He moved through the lobby the way he moved through everything — like the room had been designed for his passage and everyone in it was simply waiting to be acknowledged.

"Darling." He turned, caught her eye, drew her forward with a gesture that managed to be both loving and proprietary. "Marcus, you know my fiancée. Jane, Marcus Webb — our lead underwriter for the IPO."
Jane smiled. The smile reached her eyes. She had practiced it.
"A pleasure," she said, and meant nothing.

The next ninety minutes were a performance she could have done in her sleep. Smile at the investors. Laugh at the journalist's joke. Stand slightly behind Edward's left shoulder during the brief photo call, hand resting on his arm in precisely the way the engagement announcement had dictated. Radiant in Thornfield neutral — a slip dress the color of wet sand, hair pulled back, diamond studs at her ears. The supportive fiancée. The seal on his reinvention.
Her phone buzzed against her ribs.
*Elevator fixed. 3 PM window confirmed. —R*
Then, two minutes later:
*Story locked. 5K. Ready on your signal. —H*
And then, from a number she'd saved under a single initial:
*Jacket finished.*
Bertha.
Jane locked the screen and smiled at a woman from Le Monde who was asking about the wedding.

The fitting was a blessing. Edward assumed the spa was next, and the spa assumed she was at the fitting, and neither checked with the other because neither could imagine Jane Eyre operating outside the itinerary he had drawn for her. She changed out of the neutral dress in the car, pulled on the jeans and black sweater she'd stuffed in her tote, and gave the driver an address in the 11th.

The arrondissement didn't care about Fashion Week. It was all kebab shops and iron balconies and the particular quiet of a neighborhood that had seen revolutions before and would see them again. The building was nondescript — five stories, cream-colored, a bicycle chained to the railing. She pressed the buzzer for 4C.
Richard opened the door on the chain, saw her face, and undid it.
"You're early."
"His schedule ran ahead. Is she—"
"Here. Working."

The apartment smelled of old wood and fresh steam. The floors were parquet, original, the kind that creaked underfoot with the memory of a century's worth of tenants. Somebody's grandmother's furniture — a settee, a bookshelf, a lamp with a silk shade — gave the rooms the feel of a place that had been lived in and abandoned and lived in again. Richard had made it functional: laptop on the dining table, encrypted router blinking behind the sofa, blinds drawn.

But Jane's eyes went past all of it to the second room, where the door stood half-open, and the light — real light, afternoon Parisian light, golden and unfiltered through a window that faced south — fell across a work table, and across the woman standing at it.
Bertha Mason.

She was pinning the lapel of the punk tuxedo jacket, the fabric bunched in her left hand, a between-her-teeth pin caught between her lips. In natural light she looked nothing like the shadow Jane had seen at Thornfield. Older, yes — the lines around her eyes were deeper, her cheekbones sharper, as though the years in the attic had carved away everything soft and left only the architecture. But stronger. The tendons in her forearms stood out as she worked the fabric, and her posture was a seamstress's posture — spine straight, shoulders low, the focused calm of someone who made things with her hands.
Alive. She looked, for the first time, unmistakably alive.
She glanced up. Spat the pin into a dish.

"You're early," she said, echoing Richard, and Jane heard the echo of something else in it — the shadow of the woman who had whispered through walls, who had cut fabric in the dark. But this voice was clearer. Steadier.
"His schedule ran ahead," Jane said again.
Bertha's mouth twitched. Not quite a smile. "Sit down. We have a lot to go over."
Richard had turned the living room into a war room.

A whiteboard dominated one wall, and on it he'd mapped the Carrousel du Louvre in blue marker — the venue's floor plan, the runway, the seating rows, the camera positions for the livestream. Arrows indicated sightlines. Red dots marked exits. A timeline ran along the bottom in his small, precise handwriting:
*7:00 PM — Doors. 7:30 — Show begins. 8:20 — Final walk. 8:45 — Rochester closing speech. 9:00 — IPO announcement (projected).*
He'd written the numbers like coordinates, like a surgeon mapping an incision.

"Show schedule, venue layout, guest list, security plan, livestream feed," Richard said, standing beside the board with a laser pointer he didn't bother using. "Every camera, every exit, every window of vulnerability. The livestream goes out to four hundred million devices — phones, tablets, laptops, broadcast affiliates. Once it's live, it's live. There's no taking it back."

Jane studied the board. The Carrousel du Louvre was a glass-ceilinged hall beneath the Tuileries — grand, exposed, impossible to seal off once the crowd was inside. Edward had chosen it precisely for its theatrical scale. He wanted the world watching.
He was going to get his wish.
"The window," she said. "Between his speech and the IPO announcement."

"Fifteen minutes," Richard confirmed. "That's our kill zone. He finishes his closing remarks — the 'future of Thornfield' pitch, the gratitude, the humble-brag about the IPO — and before he gets to the announcement, you trigger the evidence."
"How?"
Richard looked at Bertha.

Bertha crossed to the laptop on the dining table, tapped a key. A schematic appeared: server architecture, access nodes, a back door mapped in green. "I built Thornfield's digital infrastructure. Every server, every firewall, every redundant backup. Edward thought he owned it because he signed the checks. But I wrote the code." Her voice was flat, matter-of-fact. "There's a trigger phrase. You send it from any device, any network, and it executes a payload that overwrites the livestream feed with the video. Simultaneously, it pushes the financial documents — the real ones, the offshore accounts, the shell company ledgers — to every connected device in the venue. And every press device. And every device streaming remotely."
"How long from trigger to full deployment?"
"Forty-seven seconds."
Jane let out a breath. Forty-seven seconds. Less than a minute to dismantle an empire.

"Helen publishes the story at the same moment," Richard continued. "I've confirmed — she has direct upload access to the Times platform. The moment her finger hits the key, the story goes live. Fully sourced, documents attached, legal vetted. And I file the complaint with the Paris prosecutor's office concurrent to the broadcast. Fraud, wire fraud, forgery, concealment of a person. The works."
"Timing is everything," Jane said.
"Timing is everything," Richard agreed.

The glow of the laptop screen threw Bertha's face into blue-white relief, and the whiteboard behind Richard caught the afternoon light like a beacon. Jane could smell the steam from the iron Bertha had been using — damp fabric, hot metal, the mineral scent of Paris tap water — and beneath it, always, the old wood of the floor, patient and creaking, holding the room together.
The buzzer sounded at four.
Richard checked the monitor, pressed the intercom. "It's Blanche."
A beat of silence. Bertha didn't move from the table, but her hands stilled on the fabric she'd been folding.
Blanche Ingram came through the door like a blade.

She was wearing black — not Thornfield black, the curated noir of the brand, but her own: a vintage YSL jacket with shoulders that could cut glass, slim trousers, boots with a wicked heel. No jewelry. No perfume Jane could detect, or maybe the perfume was so subtle it read as confidence. Her dark hair was pulled back severe and gleaming. She looked like a woman who had come to a funeral and was prepared to officiate.
She saw Richard first, nodded. Saw Jane, nodded again. Then her gaze traveled past them both to the work table, to the woman standing beside it with the punk tuxedo jacket in her hands.
Blanche stopped.

The silence was enormous. It filled the apartment the way silence fills a cathedral — reverent, terrible, heavy with everything that has ever been unsaid. Bertha looked back at her. Neither woman blinked.
Then Blanche spoke. Her voice was low, stripped of its usual music.
"I heard you. For three years. I heard the scissors. I told myself it was mice."
Her jaw tightened. "I'm sorry."
Another silence. Shorter this time. Bertha's expression didn't change, but something shifted behind her eyes — a door opening, or closing, or simply being acknowledged for the first time.
"You're here now," Bertha said. "That's what matters."

No histrionics. No excessive forgiveness. No gushing reconciliation, no tears, no embrace. Just acknowledgment. Two women who had been shaped by the same man's hands into different kinds of silence, standing in the same borrowed room, choosing the same side.
Blanche nodded once. Pulled out a chair. Sat down.
"So," she said, as though the previous two minutes hadn't happened, as though they hadn't happened at all. "Where's the plan?"

Helen's face appeared on the laptop screen at 4:30, the video call crackling with the slight delay of a London-to-Paris connection. She was in what looked like a newspaper morgue — shelves of binders behind her, a desk lamp throwing harsh light across her angular features. Her hair was pulled back and she had the bright, slightly manic look of a journalist who has been living on adrenaline and cold coffee for seventy-two hours.

"It's locked," she said without preamble. "Five thousand words, fully sourced, every document attached and verified. The offshore accounts, the shell companies, the wire transfers, the forged audit trails. But that's not the story anymore."
She leaned closer to the camera.

"I found the family. Celine Laurent's family — the designer who died in the Milan atelier fire. Her sister, her nephew. They want to talk. They've wanted to talk for years. They were told it was an accident, a faulty space heater, and when they tried to push, Thornfield's legal team buried them in NDAs and settlements with non-disparagement clauses so tight they couldn't even say her name in public."
Jane's hand found the edge of the table. The wood was rough under her fingertips.

"The story isn't corporate fraud anymore," Helen continued. "It's a system. Two decades of abuse — creative theft, financial manipulation, intimidation, concealment. At least one death. A pattern of silencing anyone who got close to the truth. And at the center of it all, one man who built a nine-figure empire on other people's genius and other people's silence."
She paused. Her smile was thin and cold and absolutely certain.
"My editor — the one who killed the story? She's going to wish she'd run it when she had the chance. Because when this drops, every outlet in the world is going to be playing catch-up to us."
"When does it run?" Richard asked.
"The moment Jane gives the signal. I have the upload queued. One click."

Jane looked around the room. Richard at the whiteboard, marker in hand. Bertha at the work table, the punk tuxedo draped over her arm like a standard. Blanche in the chair, legs crossed, watching. Helen on the screen, steady and ready.
Every thread converging.
"Good," Jane said. "Let's go through it again."

They ran the plan three more times before the light shifted and the apartment went amber, then bronze, then dark. Richard lit the one good lamp. Bertha made coffee — real coffee, from a moka pot that hissed and gurgled on a two-burner stove — and they drank it standing up, the way people drink coffee in crisis rooms, without sitting, without ceremony.
Jane went through the sequence one final time, step by step.

She would sit front row. Adèle would be beside her — Edward had insisted, the doting father, the family tableau. When Edward took the stage for his closing speech, 8:45 PM, Jane would wait. She would let him begin. Let him find his rhythm, his cadence, the practiced warmth that had sold a thousand investors on the Thornfield vision. And then, at the first pause — the breath before the IPO announcement — she would send the trigger phrase from her phone, two words that Bertha had encrypted and loaded and that would, in forty-seven seconds, overwrite the livestream with the truth.
The video. The financial documents. Helen's story. Richard's complaint. All at once. A coordinated detonation.
"And if the livestream is delayed?" Blanche asked.

"Bertha's payload targets the venue's internal network as well," Richard said. "Even if the external feed lags, every screen inside the Carrousel will display the evidence. Four hundred people in that room — buyers, editors, investors — and every one of them will see it in real time."
"Edward's phone," Jane said. "He'll have it on him. He'll see the feed drop—"

"He won't be able to stop it from the stage," Richard cut in. "There's no kill switch. I made sure of that. By the time he reaches a computer — and he won't be able to reach one without drawing attention — the evidence will be on four hundred million screens. It cannot be taken back."
Bertha set down her coffee cup. Her fingers, Jane noticed, were steady. "What if he tries to stop it another way?"
The question hung in the air. It was the question none of them had wanted to ask, because the answer required them to imagine something they had all, in their different ways, survived.
"What if he tries to hurt someone?" Jane said, giving it words.
Silence.
The lamp hummed. The floorboards settled. Somewhere in the 11th arrondissement, a scooter buzzed past and faded.
Then Adèle spoke.

She had been so quiet — sitting cross-legged on the settee in the corner, her phone dark in her lap, her dark eyes watching and absorbing every word — that Jane had nearly forgotten she was there. But she hadn't forgotten. She had been listening. And when she spoke, her voice was clear and certain in a way that made every adult in the room turn toward her.
"He won't."
Jane blinked. "Adèle—"

"He won't," she repeated, "because I'll be standing right next to you. And the cameras will be on us. He can't violence his way out of this. He can only talk his way out." She paused. Her expression was her mother's — the same high cheekbones, the same direct gaze — but the voice was entirely her own. Young, but not naive. "And he won't have anything to say."
Nobody spoke. The lamp hummed.

Jane looked at her — this girl who had grown up inside Edward's orbit, who had learned his mechanisms the way a child learns a mother tongue, not by instruction but by immersion. Who understood, perhaps better than any of them, that Edward Rochester's power had never been his hands. It had been his voice. His narrative. His ability to make the world see what he needed it to see.
And in forty-seven seconds, the world would see something else.
"Okay," Jane said quietly. "Okay."
Richard capped his marker. Bertha folded the jacket into a garment bag. Blanche reached for her coat. Helen's face on the screen nodded once, and the call ended.

Jane stood in the center of the borrowed apartment, surrounded by the tactical board and the old-wood smell and the fading afternoon light through the southern window, and felt the particular stillness of a plan that has no more room for refinement. Everything was in place. Every piece positioned. Every thread drawn taut and converging on a single point — 8:45 PM, two days from now, the Carrousel du Louvre.
Two days.

Edward was at the Plaza Athénée right now, probably charming a hedge fund manager, probably checking the IPO figures, probably touching the small of Jane's back in that proprietary way that used to feel like belonging and now felt like a leash. He thought she was at the spa. He thought everything was under control.

But Edward Rochester was a man who had built an empire on control — on the careful curation of what the world saw and what it didn't, on the silencing of women who knew too much and the disposal of people who got in the way. He had spent twenty years making sure no one could touch him.
And cornered men with empires — men who had never been denied, never been exposed, never been seen without their architecture — were the most dangerous kind of animal.
Two days.
The threads were converging. The noose was tightening. And somewhere across Paris, in a suite at the Plaza Athénée, Edward Rochester was smiling.
